subUnits.title: 10.1 Atomic Number, Mass Number and Isotopy
notes: Atomic Number (Z):
• The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom
• Unique for each element; defines the element's identity
• In a neutral atom, also equals the number of electrons
• Written as subscript before element symbol: ₆C, ₁₁Na, ₁₇Cl
Mass Number (A):
• The total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus
• Also called nucleon number (protons + neutrons = nucleons)
• Written as superscript before element symbol: ¹²C, ²³Na, ³⁵Cl
Standard Atomic Notation:
A
Z X  where X = element symbol
Examples:
• ¹²₆C: Carbon with 6 protons, 6 neutrons (12-6), 6 electrons
• ²³₁₁Na: Sodium with 11 protons, 12 neutrons (23-11), 11 electrons
• ³⁵₁₇Cl: Chlorine with 17 protons, 18 neutrons (35-17), 17 electrons
Isotopes:
• Atoms of the same element with same atomic number but different mass numbers
• Same number of protons and electrons, different number of neutrons
• Have identical chemical properties but different physical properties (mass, density)
Examples of Isotopes:
• Carbon: ¹²₆C (98.9%), ¹³₆C (1.1%), ¹⁴₆C (trace, radioactive)
• Hydrogen: ¹₁H (Protium), ²₁H (Deuterium), ³₁H (Tritium)
• Chlorine: ³⁵₁₇Cl (75%), ³⁷₁₇Cl (25%)
• Oxygen: ¹⁶₈O (99.76%), ¹⁷₈O (0.04%), ¹⁸₈O (0.20%)
Calculations with Isotopes:
For Chlorine with two isotopes:
• ³⁵Cl (75%) and ³⁷Cl (25%)
• This is why Chlorine's relative atomic mass is 35.5, not a whole number

quiz.q: The atomic number of an element represents the number of:
quiz.explanation: Atomic number (Z) is defined as the number of protons in the nucleus, which determines the element's identity.

quiz.q: The mass number of an atom is the sum of:
quiz.explanation: Mass number (A) = protons + neutrons. Electrons have negligible mass and are not included.

quiz.q: Isotopes of an element have the same:
quiz.explanation: Isotopes have the same atomic number (same protons) but different mass numbers (different neutrons).

quiz.q: For the isotope ³⁷₁₇Cl, the number of neutrons is:
quiz.explanation: Neutrons = Mass number - Atomic number = 37 - 17 = 20 neutrons.

quiz.q: Which statement about isotopes is FALSE?
quiz.explanation: Isotopes have the SAME atomic number; different atomic numbers would mean different elements.

quiz.q: The relative atomic mass of Chlorine is 35.5 because:
opts: It is an average of its isotopes' masses weighted by abundance
quiz.explanation: Chlorine has two main isotopes (³⁵Cl 75%, ³⁷Cl 25%); the weighted average is (35×0.75)+(37×0.25)=35.5.

quiz.q: Hydrogen has three isotopes. Which has the most neutrons?
quiz.explanation: Protium: 0 neutrons, Deuterium: 1 neutron, Tritium: 2 neutrons. Tritium has the most.

quiz.q: In standard notation ²³₁₁Na, the number 11 represents the:
quiz.explanation: The subscript (11) is the atomic number (protons); superscript (23) is the mass number.

quiz.q: Two atoms are isotopes if they have:
opts: Same mass number, different atomic number
Same atomic number, different mass number
quiz.explanation: Isotopes are defined as atoms with same atomic number (same element) but different mass numbers (different neutrons).

quiz.q: Calculate the relative atomic mass of element X with isotopes: ⁶⁰X (60%, mass 60) and ⁶²X (40%, mass 62).

subUnits.title: 10.2 Relative Atomic Masses Based on Carbon-12 Isotope
notes: Relative Atomic Mass (RAM):
• The average mass of an atom of an element compared to 1/12th the mass of a Carbon-12 atom
• Carbon-12 (¹²₆C) is the international standard for atomic mass measurements
• By definition: Mass of one ¹²C atom = exactly 12 atomic mass units (amu)
• 1 amu = 1/12th the mass of one ¹²C atom
Why Carbon-12 as Standard?
• Carbon forms compounds with most elements, making comparisons easy
• Carbon-12 is abundant and stable
• Provides a consistent reference point for all elements
Calculating Relative Atomic Mass:
Formula: RAM = Σ (isotope mass × fractional abundance)
Step-by-Step Method:
1. List all isotopes with their masses and percentage abundances
2. Convert percentages to decimals (divide by 100)
3. Multiply each isotope mass by its fractional abundance
4. Sum all the products to get the relative atomic mass
Worked Example 1: Chlorine
Isotopes: ³⁵Cl (75%, mass=35), ³⁷Cl (25%, mass=37)
Worked Example 2: Magnesium
Isotopes: ²⁴Mg (79%, mass=24), ²⁵Mg (10%, mass=25), ²⁶Mg (11%, mass=26)
Important Notes:
• Relative atomic masses on periodic table are weighted averages
• Most elements have RAM values that are not whole numbers due to isotopes
• For calculations, use RAM values from periodic table unless specified otherwise
• In WAEC/NECO/JAMB, questions may provide isotope data for RAM calculations

quiz.q: The standard for relative atomic mass is based on the isotope:
quiz.explanation: Carbon-12 (¹²C) is the international standard; 1 amu = 1/12th the mass of one ¹²C atom.

quiz.q: One atomic mass unit (amu) is defined as:
opts: 1/12th the mass of a Carbon-12 atom
quiz.explanation: By definition, 1 amu = 1/12 × mass of one ¹²C atom, providing a consistent reference.

quiz.q: The relative atomic mass of an element with isotopes is:
opts: The mass of the most abundant isotope
The mass of the heaviest isotope
A weighted average of isotope masses
quiz.explanation: RAM is calculated as the weighted average of all naturally occurring isotopes based on their abundances.

quiz.q: Calculate RAM for element Y: ⁷⁰Y (80%, mass 70) and ⁷²Y (20%, mass 72).
Why is the relative atomic mass of Chlorine 35.5 and not a whole number?
opts: It is an average of isotopes ³⁵Cl and ³⁷Cl
quiz.explanation: Chlorine has two main isotopes; the weighted average of their masses gives 35.5.

quiz.q: For accurate RAM calculation, isotope abundances must be expressed as:
quiz.explanation: Percentages must be converted to decimal fractions (divide by 100) before multiplying by isotope masses.

quiz.q: An element has two isotopes: A (mass 10, 20%) and B (mass 11, 80%). Its RAM is:
Which statement about relative atomic mass is TRUE?
opts: It is always a whole number
It is the mass of the most common isotope
It accounts for natural isotope abundances
quiz.explanation: RAM is a weighted average that accounts for the natural abundance of all isotopes of an element.

quiz.q: If an element has only one naturally occurring isotope, its RAM will be:
opts: A whole number equal to that isotope's mass
quiz.explanation: With only one isotope, the weighted average equals that isotope's mass (usually a whole number).

quiz.q: In WAEC/NECO exams, if isotope data is given, you should:
opts: Ignore it and use periodic table value
Calculate RAM using the given data
Assume all isotopes are equally abundant
quiz.explanation: Exam questions providing isotope data expect you to calculate RAM using the weighted average formula.
